Reviews Why It Matters Interviews 360 Playlist Readers’ Picks Weekly Email Updates Release Calendar About Us Facebook Instagram RSS Twitter Powered by Genesis The Hooters’ Eric Bazilian on Inflation and Street Gum Wisdom Tuesday, July 21, 2026 By Mayer Danzig Eric Bazilian, guitarist and singer-songwriter of The Hooters, recalls how the band’s early rehearsal space, a warehouse in a then-rough part of Philadelphia, became the birthplace of the band’s name and its first songs. He also reveals who between the band and crew gets the nicer tour bus when the Hooters are on the road. Filed Under: Interviews , Rock , Why It Matters Tagged With: Eric Bazilian , The Hooters Monday Morning Video – Austin Plaine Monday, July 20, 2026 By Mayer Danzig Some songs meet you exactly where you are — and then lift you somewhere better. Austin Plaine has a gift for that kind of winsome, quietly uplifting songwriting, and “Rise Above It” is a perfect example. It sits with the weight of hard-won experience before arriving at something that feels like grace. “Looking at the […] Filed Under: Acoustic , Americana , Singer/Songwriter , Videos Tagged With: Austin Plaine Now & Then: The Rolling Stones’ Foreign Tongues and the reach of Berry Is on Top Sunday, July 19, 2026 By Tom Osborne The Rolling Stones have spent their career making their influences easy to hear, and Chuck Berry remains one of the clearest. Foreign Tongues connects through its guitar-driven arrangements, Jagger’s character-based singing, and the band’s preference for direct, rhythm-first rock and roll. Chuck Berry’s 1959 album Berry Is on Top offers a useful point of comparison because it contains several songs and techniques that helped shape the Stones from their earliest years. Filed Under: Americana , Interviews , Rock , Why It Matters Tagged With: Jesper Lindell Monday Morning Video – Al Green and Chicago Monday, July 13, 2026 By Mayer Danzig Chicago, the rock band known for hits like “25 or 6 to 4” and “Saturday in the Park,” are joined by soul legend Al Green in this 1973 performance. Green sings his own hit “Tired of Being Alone,” with Chicago’s horns locking into the pocket behind him. Filed Under: Soul/R&B , Videos Tagged With: Al Green , Chicago Now & Then: Margo Price’s Days Of Unrest and the reach of Van Lear Rose Sunday, July 12, 2026 By Tom Osborne Margo Price has never treated country music as neutral ground, and Days Of Unrest removes any remaining doubt. Released as a July 4 weekend protest mixtape, it puts her in conversation with the folk-protest tradition while keeping one boot in country music. The “Then” album is Loretta Lynn’s Van Lear Rose, a 2004 record that made plainspoken country songwriting feel newly urgent. Steve Lacy From the fragmented breakup anthems to the formulaic moodboard fodder, nothing on Steve Lacy’s new album seems to come naturally. By Alphonse Pierre Rock Everything I Ever Saw The Menzingers Rousing paeans to wasted youth get reduced to hokey life lessons on the Pennsylvania punks’ new album, which lacks nearly all the lyrical features that made their best work so lovable. By Eli Enis Experimental Bivabippabualukka Sofie Birch Collaborating with her brother, the Copenhagen composer takes a whimsical left turn on a sprawling, pleasantly busy new album. By Shaad D’Souza Rock Bootleg The Garden On their latest album, the Shears brothers bounce from scuffed-up hardcore to breakbeat-infused rap-rock, telling tales of paranormal activity that form an impressive compendium of folklore. By Jude Noel Electronic Different When It’s Silent Tricky The trip-hop icon’s latest album is stylistically uneven, but he sounds creatively energized, particularly on brooding, desolate songs where he grapples with parental grief. By Ben Cardew See More More Reviews Experimental Tranquilizer Oneohtrix Point Never Best New Album Drawing on a cache of commercial sample CDs, Daniel Lopatin assembles an impossibly dense and transportive electronic album that takes impermanence as its inspiration. Pop/R&B Addison Addison Rae The girlypop album of summer warrants comparison to Lana, Madonna, and most of all, Britney. Are we ready for Addison? Rock Snocaps Snocaps The Crutchfield twins—accompanied by MJ Lenderman and Brad Cook—reunite for a spirited new project that plays to their strengths and embraces all the miles they’ve traveled. Pop/R&B LUX Rosalía Best New Album The Spanish singer’s fourth record is a heartfelt offering of avant-garde classical pop that roars through genre, romance, and religion. Rap Stardust Danny Brown Working with a new vanguard of outsider electronic artists, the Detroit rapper lets his freak flag fry. The results are shaky but full of heart. Pop/R&B West End Girl Lily Allen With an album that doubles as an insider’s account of a tabloid divorce, the singer finds a new evolution of her signature style: Lightness isn’t a foil for irony, but a vehicle for hurt. Rap Lil Herb G Herbo Stepping into his teenage shoes for a loose concept album, the Chicago icon reflects on the original era of drill with the clarity of a scene veteran. Electronic Repulsor Shlohmo The L.A. beatmaker turns aggressive on his fourth album—dialing up the distortion, flooding his beats with overdriven synths, and pushing anxious moods into the red. Black Sites – For Eternity Review By Dolphin Whisperer on July 17, 2026 in Reviews , Doom Metal , Heavy Metal , Progressive Metal , 49 comments “As its creators dare, modern metal music holds the unique position of having decades of realized and celebrated sounds to guide it. Whether it’s the harmonized crunch of Iron Maiden , the forlorn, minor-scale weeping of Paradise Lost , or the twisting, chiming builds of Fates Warning , classic styles like these build a world that transcends the simple attachment of a good chorus or solo. And in the lineage of NWOBHM fury, gothic sorrows, and progressive tinkering, Mark Sugar roots his long-running prog-ish, doom-ish, always heavy Black Sites project. Album after album, Sugar has explored his wide range of musical influences to capture that hard-to-attain feeling of nostalgia without ever being comfortable living in the shadow of any one band.” Metal education camp. Eaten by Sharks – The Undertow of Hate Review By ClarkKent on July 17, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , Deathcore , Technical Death Metal , 18 comments “In his review of Eaten by Sharks ’s debut, Eradication , Carcharodon mollified readers about the dangers of sharks by citing statistics. However, statistics don’t erase the fact that sharks look like the stuff of nightmaresâand they do sometimes eat people. Most of us prefer to experience this vicariously through film, where we learn sharks are quite versatile killers. They might surprise you while you’re skinny dipping and thrash you around a bit before dragging you underwater. Or one might burst through the floor of an underwater research facility and chomp you in half.” Music for chums. Novareign – Shifting the Axis of the World Review By Grin Reaper on July 16, 2026 in Reviews , Power Metal , 21 comments “Eight long years after releasing their explosive debut Legends , Novareign returns with long-awaited follow-up Shifting the Axis of the World. In his glowing review of Legends , Doc Wvrm heartily compared Novareign ’s potent shredfest to DragonForce ’s unabashed technical prowess while simultaneously praising the Los Angeles power-proggers’ songwriting chops. And he wasn’t wrongâfor over an hour, Legends unites anthemic, massively addictive power metal hooks with fret-scorching fireworks. Yet with such an incendiary debut comes dreadfully high expectations, and with such a long wait between releases and a couple of lineup changes, Novareign direly needs a worthy successor.” SuperNova? Doomcult – Sacrifice All Life Review By Steel Druhm on July 16, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , Doom Metal , 32 comments “Little Dutch doom-death band Doomcult have been lurking around for 10 years, releasing three albums that haven’t earned them a large slice of mainstream notoriety. Starting life as the solo project of one J.G. Arts, Doomcult has been a vehicle for expressing J.G.’s adoration for the classic Peaceville-era sound made famous by My Dying Bride , Anathema , and Paradise Lost . Early albums played out like a love letter to the 90s Gothic-doom sound, with long, meandering compositions wallowing in chronic sadboi dysfunction.  Fourth release, Sacrifice All Life , finds J.G. still running the show, but this time he brought in a vocalist to help round out the sound.” Cult of peace(ville). An enduring cycle of radiance, the windswept echoes of seminal Brazilian records like Clube da Esquina ripple through the record; a collage as sunny as it is meditative. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close Horse Lords :: Demand to Be Taken to Heaven Alive! Horse Lords return with what may well be their strangest and prettiest album yet. Here the Baltimore-Berlin quartet adds Protestant hymn-singing, swamp boogie, early computer music and even robot funk to their signature brand of phase-shifting krautrock groove. It is an alluring, compelling mix that plays with themes of religious devotion and utopian longing. Fifteen years into their career, Horse Lords are still making some of the most radical, captivating music of our time. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close This Heat :: This Heat Nearly a half-century after its release, This Heat’s entrancing, horrifying and ecstatic self-titled debut still lands like a vision of the end of the world, or at least the end of a world, its messages conflicting and coded but bracingly pure and full of possibility. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close Joe Gibbs & The Professionals :: African Dub All-Mighty: Chapter 3 Released in the year of clashing sevens (1977), African Dub All-Mighty: Chapter Three captures Joe Gibbs and Errol Thompson—The Mighty Two—in their finest hour, mixing up a surreal and hypnotic set that still stands tall as a pillar of ’70s dub. Working from updated Treasure Isle and Studio One riddims, Thompson transforms each track into its own sonic chamber of mystery, where thunder, synths, cuckoo clocks, telephones, and doorbells point toward the future of dub while remaining deeply rooted in its past. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close Anika :: S/T Sixteen years on, Anika still feels loosely assembled, held together by bass, space, and the chemistry between Annika Henderson, Geoff Barrow, and the wider Beak> orbit. Recorded quickly and largely live, it arrived already weathered: post-punk stripped to its framework, with vocals seemingly recorded in an adjoining room. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close Boards of Canada :: Inferno In our particulate-dense media environment, a decade-plus silence takes on the weight of a gesture in its own right—an invitation to focus, to stay awhile. Structurally, Inferno is less interested in the tension-release of deep raving than in the discursive backroads of a philosophical sci-fi novel. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close SML :: Spontaneous Music Live With Spontaneous Music Live , their new album for International Anthem, SML finally documents that experience in full: two sprawling, unedited performances recorded at Zebulon that capture the band shape-shifting in real time. Read More Spotify Apple Music Tidal Close Panda Bear and Sonic Boom :: A ? of WHEN Following the timeless Reset , Panda Bear and Sonic Boom return with A ? of WHEN , another playful act of musical curiosity. Steel drums, pedal steel, and harp loops from Mary Lattimore drift through an ever-shifting collage of samples and melodies, revealing themselves like sonic subtext. Optimistic without being naïve, it’s a record that meets experimentation with open ears and a sense of wonder. Read More Styrofoam Winos :: The Aquarium Drunkard Interview Posted on July 21, 2026 July 21, 2026 Spend a few minutes talking with Styrofoam Winos and it becomes obvious why Any River feels so effortless. There are no fixed roles, no designated leader, and very little interest in conventional band hierarchy. Just three longtime friends following songs wherever they lead. We sat down with Joe Kenkel, Trevor Nikrant, and Lou Turner to talk collaboration, improvisation, everyday poetry, and the enduring rewards of making music together. Anatolian Psych Out: Volume Two Posted on July 21, 2026 July 21, 2026 A continuation rather than a sequel, Anatolian Psych Out: Volume Two ventures deeper into one of rock’s richest parallel histories. Seventeen selections from Turkey’s extraordinary late-’60s and ’70s flowering, where centuries-old musical traditions meet fuzz guitars, electric bağlama, and hypnotic grooves—music that remains as mysterious, transportive, and untethered by time as ever. Mark & Suzann Farmer :: Dreams Posted on July 20, 2026 July 17, 2026 You can practically taste the Chivas and smell the KOOL 100s smoldering in the ashtray. Fleetwood Mac’s “Dreams,” refracted through a homemade four-track in rural Washington. Released barely a year after Rumours , Mark & Suzann Farmer’s 1978 private-press version trades California studio polish for after-hours intimacy. Radio Free Aquarium Drunkard :: July 2026 Posted on July 19, 2026 July 19, 2026 Freeform transmissions from Radio Free Aquarium Drunkard on dublab. Airing every third Sunday of the month, RFAD on dublab features the pairing of Tyler Wilcox’s Doom and Gloom from the Tomb and Chad DePasquale’s New Happy Gathering. This month, Tyler kicks it off with a mix of cosmic summer jazz, new and old. Chad follows with an hour of garage rock, psychedelic soul, outsider folk & more. Sunday, 4-6pm PT. DIY Festivals, Coast to Coast :: Ghost Canyon, Fly Around Fest & Open Melody Posted on July 17, 2026 July 17, 2026 Beyond the festival-industrial complex, a different ecosystem is quietly thriving. We check in with the organizers behind three fiercely independent gatherings—Ghost Canyon, Fly Around Fest and Open Melody—to talk curation, community, risk, and why the best music festivals still feel like beautifully assembled mixtapes rather than content farms. Michael Brook :: The Aquarium Drunkard Interview Posted on July 16, 2026 July 16, 2026 Ambient guitar pioneer Michael Brook joins us to discuss his 1992 classic Cobalt Blue, which has recently been re-issued by label 4AD with new art and a dynamic remastering job. Brook joins us in the midst of preparing to present the Cobalt Blue material live for the first time in years to discuss the record, his time on the road with John Cale, and the durability of floppy disc technology. Horse Lords :: Demand to Be Taken to Heaven Alive! Posted on July 16, 2026 July 15, 2026 Horse Lords return with what may well be their strangest and prettiest album yet. Here the Baltimore-Berlin quartet adds Protestant hymn-singing, swamp boogie, early computer music and even robot funk to their signature brand of phase-shifting krautrock groove. It is an alluring, compelling mix that plays with themes of religious devotion and utopian longing. Fifteen years into their career, Horse Lords are still making some of the most radical, captivating music of our time. The Aquarium Drunkard Show: SIRIUS/XMU (7pm PDT, Channel 35) Posted on July 15, 2026 July 14, 2026 Broadcasting from northeast Los Angeles — the Aquarium Drunkard Show on SIRIUS/XMU, channel 35. 7pm California time, Wednesdays. Trees. Gardens. Fireplaces. Corridors. Birds overhead. Sun-faded. Handmade. 34.1090° N, 118.2334° W Aaron MF Olson :: Songs Album II Posted on July 15, 2026 July 14, 2026 Starkly naming a record Songs Album II is only an appropriate detour for a musician and composer like Aaron MF Olson. Sharing a sensibility with fellow “song” records by the likes of Jim O’Rourke or Van Dyke Parks, Olson’s conscious definition of such is a moving panorama: lavish, engaging and downright clever at the same time. With its flowering avant-pop arrangements, twisting ballads and warmly inviting hooks move effortlessly alongside pastoral landscapes where exotica meets Americana. Transmissions :: Bruce Hornsby Posted on July 15, 2026 July 14, 2026 Bruce Hornsby is on the mic—and at the piano—for this special episode of the Aquarium Drunkard Transmissions podcast, joining us to discuss his latest album, Indigo Park , basketball chants, his work with The Grateful Dead and the Dead’s legacy, his taste in literary fiction, and much more. Alden Hellmuth :: Tether Posted on July 14, 2026 July 14, 2026 The second album from Alden Hellmuth doesn’t deal in aura, vibe or chill. Instead, the evocatively named NYC alto saxophonist brings in a remorseless funk drummer and two bassists, delivering a burly, sharp-cornered album of aggressive, punk-informed jazz that still makes room for melody and introspection. You could call it Blitzkrieg Bebop, if you wanted. Just don’t call it ambient. You Make The Choice :: On 30 years of Dusk at Cubist Castle Posted on July 13, 2026 July 12, 2026 What remains of the worlds that made records like this possible? On the 30th anniversary of Dusk at Cubist Castle , we gathered voices from the Aquarium Drunkard orbit to reflect on the Olivia Tremor Control’s singular masterpiece—and the disappearing ecology of regional music culture that surrounded it. Jacob Ungerleider :: Congratulations Posted on July 12, 2026 July 16, 2026 Jacob Ungerleider’s solo debut, Congratulations , finds unexpected lift in melancholy. ASSETS FOR PSYCHEDELIC WARFARE” Album Reviews , Premieres 1 Response » Jul 21 2026 (written by Islander) We have been fascinated by the unpredictable evolutions of the Italian band Viscera/// , evolutions so adventurous that it’s not an exaggeration to now call their music experimental. That’s not to say that they throw things against the wall to see what sticks, or drip ingredients into test tubes to see whether the fluids change colors or explode. They are instead quite obviously very demanding of themselves and very meticulous in their planning and execution, it’s just that they march to the beat of their own drummer; their musical interests are wide-ranging and they’ve never been satisfied by remaining in the same musical space for very long. Because of that it’s probably not hugely helpful in our introduction of their new album to provide a history of the band’s releases (and the changing musical forays they have represented) across their 26 years of activity. It’s maybe more helpful to just focus on the new music and leave the past behind. And yes, nine years after their last album, Viscera/// have a new full-length titled 4. Assets for Psychedelic Warfare that’s now set for release on July 24th by Time To Kill Records . And yes, we’re premiering it in full today. Continue reading » Posted by Islander at 9:20 am Tagged with: Viscera/// PROTEST THE HERO – WITHIN Album Reviews No Responses » Jul 21 2026 ( Andy Synn has been a fan of this band for a long, long time… but is he a fan of their new album?) There’s a commonly quoted, but often misinterpreted, maxim which states that as you grow older there comes a time when you must “put away childish things.” What it actually means is that the process of becoming an adult means growing beyond selfishness and short-sighted, self-serving impulsivity to consider the people and the world around you… but it’s often taken as a claim that one should give up the things you enjoy, your passions, your hobbies, your interests, in order to display some sort of boring, performative “maturity”. It’s a sentiment that afflicts a lot of bands too, especially when they suddenly decide to throw in an unnecessary ballad or some generically pretentious prog-parts as a signal to their audience that they’ve finally “grown up”. Luckily for all of us, however, as much as Canadian genre-splicing jesters Protest the Hero have grown and developed over the years I’m happy to report that they still possess that same shamelessly nerdy, Theatre-Kid Pop-Punk meets Dream Theatre -loving, DnD-playing, Power-Prog energy now as they did way back before they could even grow some proper beards. Continue reading » Posted by Andy Synn at 8:24 am Tagged with: Andy Synn , Protest the Hero AN NCS PREMIERE:  ARGUL — “KAMOG” Exceptions to the Rule , Premieres 1 Response » Jul 20 2026 (written by Islander) Surprises are often the spice of life, other than when the surprises involve things like getting T-boned at an intersection or having that mole turn out to be melanoma. But the song we’re about to premiere (and the whole album it comes from) has turned out to be a very happy surprise for this writer. It’s a surprise because I wasn’t previously familiar with the band, and because their style of music is outside the kind of gnashing, gnarly, macabre, and malignant brands of extremity to which I usually subject my crippled ears. And it’s a spice-of-life surprise, because it turns out to be some of the most completely captivating music I’ve heard all year. The band is Argul , a quartet from Lima, Peru, consisting of guitarist/vocalist Mauricio Guerrero , guitarist/vocalist Jose María Venturi , bassist Jian Holmes , and drummer Alonso Guerrero . They independently released a four-song demo in 2023, and now their debut album Soledad & Orgullo is set for release on August 21st by Personal Records . That album is the home of the remarkable song we’re now premiering, an enthralling track named “ Kamog “. This is not the Finnish collective that has been releasing music for the last 20 years under that name but is instead a vehicle for the solo work of Dylan Cruz from Yonkers, New York, whose resume includes current or former membership in Noxis , Scumbag , and Dissonance . Though the name may cause some confusion, no one would confuse the music of the two Hanging Gardens . The one whose song we’re premiering today traffics in a form of technically impressive and richly nuanced progressive death metal. This Hanging Garden released a debut album ( The God That Made Them Bow ) in 2024, and on September 18th Transcending Obscurity Records will release the project’s second full-length — Dream Death . The song we’re now premiering is “ Tears of Lustful, Secret Desire “. When you think “heavy music,” the small slab of New England known as Martha’s Vineyard almost certainly won’t be what comes to mind first. And if it is, I want to know where the hell you’ve been hanging out lately. But never mind that. Way back in 1999, the band There Were Wires formed in that quiet suburb, and they proceeded to play every sweaty New England club and basement venue they could crawl into. At the time, their sound was rooted in hardcore punk’s raw aggression, and they quickly found a home on Deathwish Records soon after. But after the release of their 2004 album Somnambulists , the band played what they presumed to be their final show, and the members went on to form other acts such as Doomriders , among others. Now, over 25 years later, There Were Wires have made a return that’s just as ambitious as it is unlikely. Vessel is probably a record that nobody saw coming, but despite that, it deserves your full attention.
